citations.narrative:
- An approximately 8 week old puppy was housed in an enclosure that had previously been used for rabbits. There was
old rabbit feces still present on the floor of the pen. This is a potential source of contamination to the enclosed dog. Hard
surfaces which the dog contacts must be spot cleaned daily and sanitized as needed. Dogs should not be housed in a
pen that has not been cleaned and sanitized from the previous occupant.
Correct by: 27 February 2026

- There is an approximately 8 week old puppy in the exhibit area. The facility Program of Veterinary Care does not
address dogs at all. Any dealer or exhibitor using dogs must have a Program of Veterinary Care signed by the attending
veterinarian that addresses all requirements for dogs. The facility must develop and document an appropriate Program of
Veterinary Care to meet this requirement. The PVC must be approved and signed by the facility attending veterinarian.
Correct by: 10 March 2026

There were facility problems present in more than one animal enclosure.
- A pen housing four goats had a long wire extending into the facility from the enclosure fence. This was corrected at the
time of inspection.
- A pen housing two fennec foxes has wire mesh around the interior of the cage at the floor level. The wire mesh is curled
in multiple places, and has numerous sharp points within reach of the animals.
Sharp points are a potential source of injury to enclosed animals. The facility must repair or replace all sharp points.
- An enclosure housing a sloth had an electrical multi-plug located next to the sloth's resting place, within easy reach of
the animal. The plug was live and had an extension cord plugged into it. This is a potential shock hazard to the sloth. The
facility must ensure that all electric cords are safely located to protect the enclosed animal.
- The pen housing two red kangaroos had a large hole in the rear wall within reach of the animals. This is a potential
source of injury or entrapment. The facility must ensure that all structures are maintained in good condition.

- The perimeter fence is missing multiple boards, leaving numerous gaps. This does not provide an adequate barrier and
does not restrict animals and unauthorized humans from potentially accessing the facility or making contact with the
regulated animals. The facility must repair the perimeter fence and ensure that it provides an adequate barrier.
Correct by 5 March 2026

- A cage housing 10 parakeets did not have a sufficient number of perches to accommodate all birds at the same time.
For birds that use perches, a lack of safe perches can cause stress and discomfort. The facility must ensure that the
parakeet cages contain sufficient number of appropriate perches to allow all birds in the enclosure to safely perch at the
same time.
Corrected at the time of inspection.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a facility representative.
Additional Inspectors:
MICHAEL TYGART, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n

narrative:
3.1(c)(3) 
   
Housing facilities, general. 
- An approximately 8 week old puppy was housed in an enclosure that had previously been used for rabbits. There was 
old rabbit feces still present on the floor of the pen. This is a potential source of contamination to the enclosed dog. Hard 
surfaces which the dog contacts must be spot cleaned daily and sanitized as needed. Dogs should not be housed in a 
pen that has not been cleaned and sanitized from the previous occupant. 
Correct by: 27 February 2026 
3.13(a) 
   
Veterinary care for dogs. 
- There is an approximately 8 week old puppy in the exhibit area. The facility Program of Veterinary Care does not 
address dogs at all. Any dealer or exhibitor using dogs must have a Program of Veterinary Care signed by the attending 
veterinarian that addresses all requirements for dogs. The facility must develop and document an appropriate Program of 
Veterinary Care to meet this requirement. The PVC must be approved and signed by the facility attending veterinarian. 
Correct by: 10 March 2026 
3.125(a)  Repeat
   
Facilities, general. 
There were facility problems present in more than one animal enclosure. 
- A pen housing four goats had a long wire extending into the facility from the enclosure fence. This was corrected at the 
time of inspection. 
- A pen housing two fennec foxes has wire mesh around the interior of the cage at the floor level. The wire mesh is curled 
in multiple places, and has numerous sharp points within reach of the animals. 
Sharp points are a potential source of injury to enclosed animals. The facility must repair or replace all sharp points. 
- An enclosure housing a sloth had an electrical multi-plug located next to the sloth's resting place, within easy reach of 
the animal. The plug was live and had an extension cord plugged into it. This is a potential shock hazard to the sloth. The 
facility must ensure that all electric cords are safely located to protect the enclosed animal. 
- The pen housing two red kangaroos had a large hole in the rear wall within reach of the animals. This is a potential 
source of injury or entrapment. The facility must ensure that all structures are maintained in good condition.

3.127(d) 
   
Facilities, outdoor. 
- The perimeter fence is missing multiple boards, leaving numerous gaps. This does not provide an adequate barrier and 
does not restrict animals and unauthorized humans from potentially accessing the facility or making contact with the 
regulated animals. The facility must repair the perimeter fence and ensure that it provides an adequate barrier. 
Correct by 5 March 2026 
3.153(a)(2) 
   
Primary enclosures. 
- A cage housing 10 parakeets did not have a sufficient number of perches to accommodate all birds at the same time. 
For birds that use perches, a lack of safe perches can cause stress and discomfort. The facility must ensure that the 
parakeet cages contain sufficient number of appropriate perches to allow all birds in the enclosure to safely perch at the 
same time. 
Corrected at the time of inspection. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a facility representative. 
 
Additional Inspectors: 
MICHAEL TYGART, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n
